logstash-logging-spring-boot-starter/pom.general_idea: Update the Java version property from 1.8 to 11 to target the new JDK.
Replace the removed javax.annotation-api dependency with jakarta.annotation-api to maintain access to standard annotations in Java 11.
logstash-logging-spring-boot-starter/pom.why_exists: javax.annotation-api is no longer included in the JDK module system starting from Java 9, and the artifact may be unavailable or incompatible without explicit migration to Jakarta EE namespaces.

logstash-logging-spring-boot-starter/pom.general_idea: Switch maven-compiler-plugin configuration from separate source/target to the unified release flag for better cross-compilation support in Java 9+.

pom.xml.general_idea: Set the Maven compiler release property to 11 to target Java 11 bytecode.
pom.xml.why_exists: Without this change, the project continues to compile against the default source/target level (likely Java 8), failing to produce Java 11 compatible bytecode or utilize Java 11 language features.

reactive-logstash-logging-spring-boot-st.general_idea: Update the Java version property from 1.8 to 11 to target the new JDK.
reactive-logstash-logging-spring-boot-st.why_exists: The project must compile against Java 11 APIs and bytecode; keeping 1.8 would result in incompatible class files or missing API support.

reactive-logstash-logging-spring-boot-st.general_idea: Replace the javax.annotation-api dependency with jakarta.annotation-api to align with the Jakarta EE namespace migration required for modern Spring Boot/JDK 11+ environments.
reactive-logstash-logging-spring-boot-st.why_exists: The javax.annotation package is no longer provided by default in JDK 11+ (removed from java.se.ee module), causing NoClassDefFoundError or compile errors if not explicitly provided via the Jakarta namespace.

reactive-logstash-logging-spring-boot-st.general_idea: Switch maven-compiler-plugin configuration from separate source/target settings to the unified release flag.
reactive-logstash-logging-spring-boot-st.why_exists: The release flag is the standard way to specify Java version in JDK 9+ and ensures consistent compilation and runtime compatibility; while source/target might still work, release is the intended modern approach.
